title: Granny hexagon
subtitle: Six corners, three rounds, the same cluster logic as the square
excerpt: The granny square's hexagonal cousin. Six corners instead of four, three trebles between each corner cluster, three rounds to a 10 cm motif. Hexagons tessellate without leftover gaps — the ideal motif for blankets, throws, and bags built up from many small pieces.
sourceNotes: Weldon's Practical Crochet, Volume 4 (1888) — hexagonal motif worked from a centre ring with six corners (Internet Archive). Therese de Dillmont, Encyclopedia of Needlework (1886) — hexagon construction logic and chain-spacing cross-reference (Project Gutenberg #20776). Modern join-as-you-go reference adapted from open-access crochet-journal articles on hexagon tessellation.

crochet.gaugeText: Each finished hexagon measures 10 cm flat-side to flat-side (about 11.5 cm corner-to-corner) in DK-weight cotton with a 4 mm hook, after a light steam-block.
crochet.finishedSizeText: Each motif: 10 cm flat-to-flat (11.5 cm corner-to-corner). The pattern below makes one motif; multiply for throws, bags, or cushion covers.

chartDefinition.title: Granny hexagon — all three rounds
chartDefinition.caption: The chart reads outward from the magic ring at the centre. Six corner chain-2 spaces sit between corner cluster pairs; side chain-1 spaces sit between cluster pairs along the sides.

recipeTools.notes: Useful on round 3 to mark the start of the round.

glossaryTerms.definition: How shapes fit together without gaps. Hexagons tessellate (each hexagon shares an edge with six neighbours, leaving no holes); squares tessellate; pentagons don't. The reason hexagonal motifs make particularly clean multi-motif blankets.

content.text: The granny hexagon is the granny square's hexagonal cousin — same magic ring, same 3-treble clusters, same chain-2 corners. The only difference is the number of corners: six instead of four. Three rounds make a 10 cm hexagon (flat-side to flat-side); the construction logic transfers directly from the granny square, so anyone who's worked one can work the other without retraining.
Hexagons
tessellate

— they fit together without gaps. That makes them particularly clean for blankets and bags built from many small motifs: no leftover triangles to fill the corners, no awkward border ratio. A throw of 60 hexagons makes a soft honeycomb 90 × 100 cm; a market-bag panel of 12 hexagons makes a 30 × 25 cm rectangle.

If the granny square is your first multi-motif piece, work three or four hexagons first to internalise the cluster logic. The hexagon adds two corner clusters and two side spaces per round — slightly more counting than the square, slightly easier to spot a missed corner because the piece quickly becomes obviously asymmetric.

attrs.title: Materials
attrs.body: For one motif: about 6 g of DK-weight cotton. 4 mm crochet hook (UK 8, US G/6). Tapestry needle. Scissors. Optional: a stitch marker for marking the start of round 3.

content.text: Gauge

Each finished hexagon measures 10 cm flat-side to flat-side (about 11.5 cm corner-to-corner) in DK-weight cotton with a 4 mm hook, after a light steam-block. If your motif is smaller, try a 4.5 mm hook; if larger, try a 3.5 mm. Hexagons are more forgiving of tension variation than squares — a 5% gauge error on a hexagon still tessellates with its neighbours, where on a square the same error compounds into a wonky blanket.
Stitches used
chain (ch) — UK and US shared.
slip stitch (sl st) — UK and US shared.
treble (tr) — UK convention; the same stitch as US "double crochet".
magic ring (MR).
Pattern

Worked in the round, joined at the end of each round with a slip stitch into the top of the starting chain. Right side faces you throughout — do not turn. Numbers in brackets at the end of each round are the running stitch count.
The 3-chain turning chain at the start of each round counts as the first treble of the first cluster.
Round 1

Form a magic ring. Ch 3 (counts as the first tr). Work 2 tr into the ring. *Ch 2, 3 tr into the ring*; repeat from * to * four more times. Ch 2, sl st to top of beginning ch-3 to join. Pull the tail to close the ring. (18 tr, 6 ch-2 corner spaces.)
Round 2

Sl st across the top of the next 2 tr and into the first ch-2 corner sp. Ch 3 (counts as the first tr). Into the same ch-2 corner sp, work 2 tr, ch 2, 3 tr. *Ch 1, into the next ch-2 corner sp work (3 tr, ch 2, 3 tr)*; repeat from * to * four more times. Ch 1, sl st to top of beginning ch-3 to join. (36 tr, 6 ch-2 corner spaces, 6 ch-1 side spaces.)
Round 3

Sl st across the top of the next 2 tr and into the first ch-2 corner sp. Ch 3 (counts as the first tr). Into the same ch-2 corner sp, work 2 tr, ch 2, 3 tr. Ch 1. *Into the next ch-1 side sp, work 3 tr. Ch 1. Into the next ch-2 corner sp, work (3 tr, ch 2, 3 tr). Ch 1.* Repeat from * to * four more times. Into the last ch-1 side sp, work 3 tr. Ch 1. Sl st to top of beginning ch-3 to join. (54 tr, 6 ch-2 corner spaces, 12 ch-1 side spaces.)
Chart

The chart shows all three rounds at once, in working order from the centre outward. Read each round anti-clockwise from the slip-stitch join. The six corner clusters meet at chain-2 spaces; side clusters meet at chain-1 spaces. Round 3 carries the abbreviation "3tr × 18" because the pattern repeats — eighteen 3-treble clusters around the round, alternating chain-1 (side) and chain-2 (corner) spaces.

definition.title: Granny hexagon — all three rounds
definition.caption: Six corner chain-2 spaces sit between corner cluster pairs; side chain-1 spaces sit between cluster pairs along the sides.

content.text: Finishing

Fasten off. Leave a 15 cm tail. Sew in both tails along the back of the piece with a tapestry needle. Block lightly (wet or steam, your preference) so the six sides sit flat and the corners reach their points.
Joining hexagons into a finished piece

Lay the blocked hexagons in the honeycomb arrangement you want before joining. The classic tessellation is offset rows: row 1 has six hexagons in a line, row 2 has six hexagons shifted half-a-hexagon to the right, row 3 returns to row 1's alignment, and so on. The shifted rows fill the V-shaped gaps between the hexagons of the row above and below.

Whip-stitch joining works well for hexagons: with a tapestry needle and matching yarn, sew through the back loops only along one edge of two adjacent hexagons. Six edges per hexagon means six joining seams per hexagon (less the ones along the outside of the finished piece). Allow about 50 cm of yarn per joining edge.
Care
DK cotton washes at 30°C on a wool-or-handwash cycle, or by hand. Don't tumble-dry. Lay the finished piece flat on a clean towel and reshape so the hexagons stay flat and aligned.
What to try next

Four rounds of the same construction makes a 15 cm hexagon; five rounds makes a 20 cm hexagon large enough to back a small cushion. Multi-colour hexagons follow the same pattern with a colour change at the start of each round — fasten off the previous round's yarn before slipping into the next round's first chain-space, and join in the new colour with a standing treble. The Victorian Weldon's hexagon doily is the same three-round construction with a fourth round of lace — alternating shells and picots — that gives the motif a decorative edge instead of a clean joinable side.
